questions.text: I instinctively offer my seat, my place in line, or my turn to someone else.
I do not usually detect when someone drops a hint about what they want for a gift or occasion.
I notice when a group's unspoken topic of avoidance is the most important thing no one is saying.
I enjoy being the gatekeeper who controls access to key decisions.
I often make purchases on impulse when something catches my eye.
I find that creating checklists helps me stay focused and avoid overlooking details.
Even a mildly critical tone of voice can set off alarm bells throughout my system.
I find that I learn about social norms through trial and error rather than intuition.
I hold the view that processing suffering through analytical frameworks is more productive than processing it through emotional engagement.
I would trade a comfortable retirement for the chance to fund someone else's education.
I routinely choose quality-of-life improvements for myself over gifts for others.
I tend to let the group define its own evaluation criteria.
I believe that careful planning is the foundation of meaningful accomplishment.
I feel that accumulating things for myself is less important than ensuring others are provided for.
I experience the minutes before a big event as an almost electric buzzing under my skin.
My emotional responses are present but proportionate — they don't take over.
I tend to take jokes and comments literally when others see layers of meaning.
I find that my emotional responses are like a dimmer switch set to moderate rather than full brightness.
I volunteer to chair committees or working groups without hesitation.
When I see others in pain, I feel a moral pull to respond that outweighs my own comfort.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the conviction that, once fully internalized, transforms not just specific decisions but the entire orientation of a person's life.
I don't dwell on things I've forgotten to do; I simply pick them up when I remember.
I notice when someone positions a request as a suggestion to make it more palatable.
I develop contingency plans for critical tasks even when things are going well.
I hold the view that personal fulfillment should not be sacrificed for the sake of others.
I believe that decisions about how to respond to suffering should be based on evidence and reasoning rather than on the strength of the emotional response it provokes.
I detect when a gift or favor comes with unspoken strings attached.
I travel light and improvise with whatever is available if I need something.
I feel that collaborative decision-making leads to more durable outcomes.
I find that treating painful situations as analytical challenges rather than emotional ones is the approach that lets me contribute the most.
I feel at home being the primary representative of a group to the outside world.
I hold the view that my nervous system is exceptionally sensitive to stimulation.
I experience daily frustrations — traffic, delays, minor inconveniences — without a noticeable rise in my stress level.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is not diminished by distance, familiarity, or circumstance, and my response should reflect that.
I prefer that quality standards be maintained through peer review rather than a single gatekeeper.
I hold the view that self-improvement books, courses, and retreats are among my best investments.
